competitors.label: ARIA roles
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical MDN reference for ARIA roles and how they map meaning into the accessibility tree.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on the role taxonomy and how ARIA roles communicate semantics to assistive technologies.
theirGaps: The reference treatment is less direct about the common production mistake of replacing correct native HTML with ARIA that still lacks keyboard behavior and state updates.

realWorldUseCases: If you are building a custom menu button, tabs widget, or combobox that native HTML cannot express on its own, ARIA can expose the missing semantics.
followUpConfusion: Use native HTML first. Reach for ARIA only when native semantics are missing, and treat keyboard behavior, focus handling, and state updates as separate implementation work.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains what roles are for, but our answer is now more grounded in the real custom-widget scenario where ARIA is justified.
actionableExamples: It remains a strong reference page, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page now handles the crucial follow-up more directly: ARIA changes semantics, not keyboard behavior or state management.

actionableExamples: Keep the native-button-versus-div-role-button contrast near the opening so the do-versus-don't example stays obvious.

competitors.winReason: MDN is authoritative on role taxonomy, but our page is now more useful for interviews and production reasoning because it turns ARIA into a native-first decision and a custom-widget responsibility model.

competitors.label: HTML Interview Questions and Answers
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's HTML interview bank is directly relevant because ARIA, accessibility, and semantic HTML are core HTML interview themes.
theirStrengths: It is a strong broad HTML interview-prep source.
theirGaps: The category page is less focused on the exact native-first versus custom-widget trade-off in ARIA usage.

realWorldUseCases: If you are building a custom menu button, tabs widget, or combobox that native HTML cannot express on its own, ARIA can expose the missing semantics.
followUpConfusion: Use native HTML first. Reach for ARIA only when native semantics are missing, and treat keyboard behavior, focus handling, and state updates as separate implementation work.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd is relevant at the HTML interview level, but the landing page is less anchored to the exact custom-widget accessibility scenario.
actionableExamples: It remains a competitive worked-answer interview source, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our answer now exposes the native-first rule and the semantics-versus-behavior distinction more directly than the category-level interview bank.

actionableExamples: Keep the custom-widget example and the native-first rule tightly coupled so the page stays easy to skim.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is broad interview prep, but our page is more specific about the ARIA failure mode interviewers usually care about: roles without the behavior and state model that make the control actually usable.

competitors.label: ARIA and HTML
competitors.selectionReason: web.dev has a topic-direct ARIA and HTML guide with explicit native-first rules, keyboard requirements, and concrete do/don't examples.
theirStrengths: It is very strong on native-first guidance, keyboard accessibility rules, and concrete do-versus-don't code examples.
theirGaps: It is broader and more tutorial-like than a short trivia answer.

actionableExamples: still needs Enter/Space handlers, visible focus styling, and correct aria-pressed updates to behave like a real button.
followUpConfusion: Use native HTML first. Reach for ARIA only when native semantics are missing, and treat keyboard behavior, focus handling, and state updates as separate implementation work.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev is still strong on grounding ARIA in the accessibility tree and custom-control constraints, so the real-world criterion stays close.
actionableExamples: Our paired native-versus-custom control example is now more compact and directly actionable for the common interview follow-up.
followUpConfusion: Our page now answers the likely next question more directly by spelling out that semantics are not the same as keyboard behavior or state updates.

realWorldUseCases: If this page needs a later pass, add one slightly richer tabs or menu-button example without growing the answer too much.

competitors.winReason: web.dev is a strong tutorial source, but our page is now better as a short interview answer because the native-versus-custom example and the semantics-versus-behavior rule answer the two follow-ups people hit first.
